unit.name: Generative multimodal foundation models and training practice
unit.description: Explains the shift to generative multimodal systems, frozen-language-model adapters, Flamingo-style architectures, and related practical trade-offs.
unit.summary: The lecture next shifts from contrastive/discriminative models to generative ones such as SimVLM and CoCa, arguing that caption generation and decoder-based objectives can yield richer representations than pure matching losses [ts=2441s][ts=2455s]. Kiela then describes the frozen-language-model recipe: keep a strong LM fixed, learn projections into its token space, and exploit in-context learning behavior for multimodal grounding [ts=2486s][ts=2528s]. Flamingo is presented as a key example: a frozen Chinchilla LM is paired with a vision encoder, perceiver resampler, and gated cross-attention so multiple images can condition generation, while BLIP-2 shows a similar projection strategy with OPT or FlanT5 [ts=2563s][ts=2640s][ts=2756s]. The unit closes with practical Q&A points: frozen layers preserve pretrained abilities, earlier fusion usually yields richer understanding if affordable, and unified models like FLAVA are motivated partly by robustness to missing modalities [ts=2944s][ts=3009s][ts=3256s].

key_points.text: Generative multimodal models such as SimVLM and CoCa add decoders and caption-generation objectives to learn richer representations.
A strong recipe is to freeze the language model and learn a projection from visual features into the language-model token space.
Few-shot multimodal grounding can emerge when a frozen LM receives projected visual inputs plus in-context examples.
Flamingo uses a vision encoder, perceiver resampler, and gated cross-attention before a frozen Chinchilla language model.
BLIP-2 learns a mapping between an image encoder and large language models such as OPT or FlanT5.
Multimodal chain-of-thought prompting improves reasoning benchmarks like ScienceQA by asking the model to generate a rationale first.
Freezing means gradients may flow through a module, but its weights are not updated so pretrained abilities are preserved.
Earlier fusion is usually better for richer multimodal understanding, while late fusion is cheaper and easier to train.

kp_catalog.name: Generative multimodal language models
kp_catalog.description: Decoder-centric multimodal systems that condition large language models on visual inputs through projections, cross-attention, or adapter-like components. Vision-language models condition an autoregressive language model on image tokens so visual tasks can be handled through text generation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because generative multimodal language models formalizes how decoder-centric multimodal systems that condition large language models on visual inputs through projections, cross-attention, or adapter-like components, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between capstone and gateway and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Multimodal fusion strategies
kp_catalog.description: The choice of when and how modalities interact, including early, middle, and late fusion, plus modulation and attention-based schemes.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because multimodal fusion strategies formalizes how the choice of when and how modalities interact, including early, middle, and late fusion, plus modulation and attention-based schemes, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Unified multimodal foundation models
kp_catalog.description: The idea that one jointly pretrained model should handle unimodal and multimodal tasks rather than requiring separate systems.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because unified multimodal foundation models synthesizes the idea that one jointly pretrained model should handle unimodal and multimodal tasks rather than requiring separate systems into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.
